narrative:
Congressman Daniel Goldman arrived in Washington with the kind of resume that makes for great television and complicated governance. As lead counsel in Donald Trump's first impeachment, he became a household name among MSNBC viewers. As heir to the Levi Strauss fortune with a net worth between $64 million and $253 million, he became one of the wealthiest members of Congress [1]. He won his 2022 primary in New York's 10th district by pouring over $4 million of his own money into the race, prompting rivals to accuse him of trying to purchase the seat outright [2]. The voters of Lower Manhattan and brownstone Brooklyn apparently decided that was fine.

Goldman campaigned on banning congressional stock trading — a position that aged like milk left on the Capitol steps. Within months of being sworn in, financial disclosures revealed he had made over 480 individual stock trades, far outpacing most of his colleagues [3]. The punchline arrived when reports surfaced that Goldman sold shares of First Republic Bank days before it received a government bailout, and dumped PacWest Bancorp stock two days before it cratered during the regional banking crisis [4]. His office insisted an independent financial advisor managed the trades and that a blind trust was in progress. The trust was eventually finalized, but not before Goldman had racked up 639 trades — making him one of Congress's most active traders while simultaneously cosigning letters demanding a trading ban [5].

To his credit, Goldman has done genuine legislative work on issues that align with the 80% Bill. He co-introduced the FAMILY Act with Senator Gillibrand and Rep. DeLauro to establish 12 weeks of national paid family leave [6]. He has been one of the House's loudest voices against junk fees, introducing the End Junk Fees for Renters Act and holding press conferences highlighting the $65 billion annual cost of hidden charges to American consumers [7]. He serves on the Judiciary and Homeland Security committees, where his prosecutorial background gives him a sharper edge than most during hearings.

But Goldman's fundamental problem is the gap between his populist messaging and his personal financial reality. He represents a district that includes Wall Street — literally — and his top donors include Goldman Sachs ($58,000 through his Victory Fund) and billionaire real estate developers like Douglas Durst and Stephen Ross [8]. He received $184,000 from AIPAC-linked donors in the 2023-2024 cycle. When you are worth a quarter-billion dollars, campaigning on economic fairness requires a level of self-awareness that Goldman has not always demonstrated. He is a talented questioner, a committed Democrat, and living proof that in American politics, the loudest advocates for banning stock trading are sometimes the ones doing the most of it.

Marie Gluesenkamp Perez has built a brand as the grease-under-her-fingernails, auto-shop-owning Democrat who is nothing like those other Washington politicians. And to be fair, she has co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading (the TRUST in Congress Act) and introduced the No Corruption in Government Act to triple the lobbying ban for departing members. On paper, that checks off stock trading, lobbying bans, and even pay raises in one bill. Impressive range for a Blue Dog.

But the Blue Dog label is where things get interesting. As co-chair of the Blue Dog Coalition, Perez has positioned herself as the kind of moderate Democrat who crosses the aisle so frequently she might need an E-ZPass. She voted for the Laken Riley Act, the SAVE Act (twice), and even joined Republicans in censuring fellow Democrat Al Green. Her top campaign donor in 2023-2024? AIPAC, at $190,401, followed by EMILY's List at $129,540 and Microsoft at $90,836. For someone who campaigns on being a working-class populist, that donor list reads more like a K Street power lunch menu.

On the 80% Bill's platform, Perez is a mixed bag of genuine reform instincts and convenient absences. She has pushed to ban stock trading and extend lobbying prohibitions, and she co-introduced election reform legislation including proposals for multi-member districts and independent redistricting commissions. But on taxing the ultra-wealthy, Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, and funding Social Security, her Blue Dog deficit-hawk instincts keep her safely on the sidelines. She has not co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act or any dark money ban, despite representing a district where voters overwhelmingly support transparency in elections.

The real tension in Perez's record is the gap between her populist branding and her donor relationships. She talks about "delivering for your community, not enriching yourself," while her campaign is bankrolled by one of the largest corporate-aligned PACs in American politics. She supports fair elections in press releases while the Blue Dog PAC that sustains her caucus takes money from pharmaceutical companies and defense contractors. It is the political equivalent of putting a "Support Local" bumper sticker on a car financed by a multinational bank.

To her credit, Perez does put points on the board where many Democrats in swing districts would not dare. The stock trading ban is real, the lobbying extension is real, and her willingness to work across the aisle on structural reform deserves acknowledgment. But until she reconciles her populist image with her donor base and takes positions on the majority of the 80% Bill's platform, she remains a politician whose actions are carefully calibrated to survive in a Trump +15 district rather than to champion the supermajority consensus.

Rep. Craig Goldman is a fifth-generation Fort Worth Texan, a twelve-year veteran of the Texas state legislature, and the proud owner of up to $5.5 million in ExxonMobil stock and up to $1 million in Diamondback Energy shares. Naturally, the House Republican leadership placed him on the Energy and Commerce Committee, where his Energy Subcommittee oversees fossil fuel exploration, pipelines, and the strategic petroleum reserve. If you designed a conflict of interest in a laboratory, it would look exactly like Craig Goldman's committee assignment.

Goldman chaired the Texas House Energy Resources Committee during the 2021 and 2023 sessions, where he "worked to strengthen the Texas oil and gas industry." He has carried that same mission to Congress with the Homeowner Energy Freedom Act, which repeals provisions of the Inflation Reduction Act that promoted energy-efficient home appliances. The bill passed the House 210-199. Goldman framed it as protecting "consumer choice," which is a creative way to describe eliminating incentives for appliances that save consumers money on their energy bills — money that might otherwise flow to the oil and gas companies whose stock fills his portfolio.

Goldman also introduced the Zero-Based Regulatory Budgeting to Unleash American Energy Act of 2026, because apparently the energy industry is being held back by too much regulation. His ExxonMobil dividends — between $50,000 and $100,000 annually — presumably agree.

On the 80% Bill's 20 policies, Goldman's record is a masterclass in selective populism. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, which is remarkable for a man whose stock portfolio is a walking advertisement for why such a ban exists. On Pentagon audits, term limits, the DISCLOSE Act, dark money bans, and corporate PAC restrictions, Goldman offers the silence of a man who sees no problem with the system that elevated him.

As a real estate investment partner, Goldman has no record of supporting bans on corporate home buying or farmland protections. On Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, junk fee bans, and the right to repair, the congressman from Fort Worth has contributed precisely nothing.

The voters of TX-12 — spanning Fort Worth, Parker County, and parts of Tarrant County — overwhelmingly support the 80% Bill's reforms. They deserve a representative who legislates for their kitchen tables, not for his stock portfolio. Goldman earns six figures in oil dividends while voting on oil policy. That's not representation. That's a business arrangement.

Rep. Laura Gillen arrived in Congress in January 2025 as the Democrat who flipped New York's 4th District by promising to be a "common-sense" moderate -- and she has spent her first term proving that "common sense" in Washington mostly means triangulating furiously while hoping nobody checks the receipts. Her very first act was voting with Republicans on the Laken Riley Act, which she proudly called "a bipartisan bill to keep our communities safe" [1]. She then joined all Republicans in censuring fellow Democrat Al Green for speaking during Trump's State of the Union [2]. The moderate brand was established early and defended aggressively.

Gillen earned the endorsement of End Citizens United, which praised her as "a strong pro-democracy, anti-corruption champion" who refuses corporate PAC money [3]. She has voiced support for the DISCLOSE Act and the Freedom to Vote Act [4]. These are genuinely good positions on the 80% Bill scorecard. But here is where it gets delicious: while campaigning as the anti-dark-money candidate, Gillen drew a $40,000 "fellow" salary from Our American Future Foundation, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit bankrolled by George Soros's political network [5]. The Foundation for Accountability and Civic Trust filed an FEC complaint alleging these payments to future candidates violated federal law [6]. Gillen's campaign also benefited from $500,000 that Soros's Democracy PAC funneled to the League of Conservation Voters Victory Fund, which then spent heavily on independent expenditures backing her candidacy [7]. Opposing dark money while benefiting from it is the kind of cognitive dissonance that keeps political satirists employed.

On healthcare, Gillen has positioned herself as a defender of Medicare and Social Security [8], and she blasted the One Big Beautiful Bill Act for its Medicaid cuts, warning that medical costs would "skyrocket" for Long Islanders [9]. She voted against the final bill [10]. Her committee assignments -- Transportation and Infrastructure, plus Science, Space, and Technology [11] -- are fine for a freshman but give her no direct leverage on the financial reform, healthcare, or anti-corruption bills that comprise the 80% Bill's core. She has introduced legislation on aviation weather safety and child car seat education [12], which are admirable but hardly the stuff of structural reform.

The biggest gap in Gillen's record is what she has not done. There is no cosponsorship of bills to ban congressional stock trading, audit the Pentagon, establish paid family leave, close the carried interest loophole, or impose congressional term limits. For a member endorsed by End Citizens United, the absence of action on banning corporate purchase of single-family homes or funding Social Security is conspicuous. She talks a reform game on campaign finance but has yet to translate that rhetoric into a broader populist agenda.

Rep. Brandon Gill, the youngest House Republican in the 119th Congress, arrived in Washington in January 2025 with a resume that reads like a Silicon Valley fever dream: Dartmouth economics degree, investment banking stint, hedge fund analyst, media company founder, and film producer. He received Trump's endorsement within two weeks of announcing his candidacy, joined the Freedom Caucus on day one, and was promptly placed on the Judiciary, Budget, and Oversight Committees — including the DOGE Subcommittee led by Marjorie Taylor Greene. The man is nothing if not efficient at networking.

Gill's commitment to "eradicating government waste" through the DOGE Subcommittee is a fascinating juxtaposition with his personal financial conduct. By November 2025, Gill had purchased between $1.15 million and $2.6 million in Bitcoin — making him Congress's most prolific crypto buyer. His first purchase came days after Trump signed an executive order promoting digital asset deregulation. His February buy occurred just before Trump announced a "strategic Bitcoin reserve." At least two of these trades were disclosed weeks past the 45-day STOCK Act deadline, earning him a violation of the very financial transparency law he's supposed to uphold. The fine? A laughable $200. The man railing against government waste apparently considers financial disclosure laws a waste of his time.

When asked about banning congressional stock trading — a policy supported by over 80% of Americans — Gill's silence is deafening. He sits on the Oversight Committee that literally held a hearing on Pentagon audit failures, yet he hasn't pushed for the audit reforms the 80% Bill demands. On term limits, dark money, corporate PAC bans, and the DISCLOSE Act, the youngest Republican in Congress has the legislative record of a retiree.

Gill's 45% of sponsored legislation focuses on immigration — the Somalia Immigration Moratorium Act, the Student Visa Integrity Act — while 36% targets "government operations." What he hasn't introduced is anything resembling the 20 policies that 80% of Americans actually want: no Medicare drug negotiation bills, no paid family leave proposals, no junk fee bans, no right-to-repair legislation, no farmland protection, no action on corporate home buying.

The voters of TX-26 in Denton County sent Gill to Washington with 62.8% of the vote. They deserve a representative who fights for the supermajority consensus, not one who trades Bitcoin on insider timing while lecturing Democrats about fiscal responsibility.

Rep. Maggie Goodlander arrived in Congress in January 2025 with a resume so polished it practically reflects light. A Yale undergrad and Yale Law grad [1], she clerked for Merrick Garland and Supreme Court Justice Stephen Breyer [2], served as a Navy Reserve intelligence officer [3], helped write the constitutional case for Trump's first impeachment [4], and then took a turn busting monopolies as a Deputy Assistant Attorney General in the DOJ's Antitrust Division [5]. She is also married to Jake Sullivan, Biden's National Security Advisor, a detail her campaign materials treat like a footnote but her donor rolls treat like a headline [6].

About those donors. Goodlander raised over $1.5 million in her first two months as a candidate, with 88 percent of individual contributions coming from outside New Hampshire [7] -- more than double the median out-of-state rate for House incumbents, and she was not even an incumbent. Her top industry? Securities and investment, at $847,525 [8]. AIPAC chipped in over $60,000 [9]. Her individual donors include employees of Meta, Palantir, Blackstone, and various white-shoe law firms [7]. A super PAC linked to Jeff Bezos and Michael Bloomberg spent over $150,000 boosting her candidacy [7]. For someone who built her career prosecuting corporate monopolies, the irony of being bankrolled by the finance and tech elite is, shall we say, structurally robust.

In Congress, Goodlander has been a model party soldier: she has missed zero of 449 roll call votes and voted against the Democratic majority exactly zero times [10]. She launched a "Lowering Costs Caucus" [11] and introduced the Corporate Crimes Against Health Care Act, which would impose up to six years in prison on executives who loot healthcare entities like nursing homes when patients die as a result [12]. She co-introduced the 3D Printed Gun Safety Act [13] and has championed universal background checks and an assault weapons ban [14]. She also joined Senator Elizabeth Warren in pushing legislation targeting private equity abuse in healthcare [12].

The profile is impressive on paper and perfectly orthodox in practice. Goodlander has not cast a single vote that would make her Wall Street donors nervous, nor has she broken with party leadership on any recorded vote. She talks a sharp game on antitrust while collecting checks from the industries she once investigated. Whether she is a reformer constrained by the system or a system player fluent in the language of reform depends on which version of her resume you are reading -- the DOJ one or the OpenSecrets one.

Adam Gray is the freshman Democrat representing California's 13th congressional district, a Central Valley swing seat he won by a razor-thin 187 votes in 2024 after losing the same race by a similarly slim margin two years earlier [1]. A fifth-generation Merced native who grew up working in his family's dairy supply and feed store, Gray spent a decade in the California State Assembly before making the jump to Congress, where he now serves on the Agriculture and Natural Resources committees and holds the title of whip of the Blue Dog Coalition [2]. He has built his brand on bipartisan bona fides -- founding a Problem Solvers Caucus in Sacramento and positioning himself as a centrist willing to buck his party when the political math demands it [3].

On the substance of the 80% Bill, Gray's record is thin but revealing. He was one of just six House Democrats to cross the aisle and vote to end the 43-day government shutdown in 2025, arguing that hungry families shouldn't be held hostage to partisan brinkmanship [4]. He also joined 45 other Democrats in voting for the Republican-backed Laken Riley Act [5]. These votes cement his maverick image, but on the actual policies that command 80% public support -- banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, taxing the ultra-wealthy, Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, congressional term limits -- Gray's public record is largely a blank page. He has not co-sponsored major legislation on any of these fronts. For a self-described champion of the people, the silence is notable.

Where Gray gets truly interesting is in the follow-the-money department. His campaign website proudly declares he is "not funded by corporate PAC interests" [6], but his role as Blue Dog whip puts him at the center of a far more creative fundraising apparatus. In June 2025, Gray appeared alongside Blue Dog co-chair Marie Gluesenkamp Perez at "WelcomeFest," an event where the coalition announced plans to launch a new super PAC to accept unlimited donations and a new dark money nonprofit that would not be required to disclose its funders [7]. The Blue Dog PAC itself has historically drawn heavily from the energy, financial services, and healthcare industries, which accounted for over half of its receipts in prior cycles [8]. So while Gray may keep corporate PAC money at arm's length from his campaign account, he is actively helping build the institutional plumbing for precisely the kind of undisclosed, unlimited corporate spending that the 80% Bill's provisions on dark money and the DISCLOSE Act are designed to eliminate.

The bottom line: Adam Gray is a survival specialist in one of America's most competitive districts, and his legislative identity is still being written. He talks the populist talk of a Central Valley farm kid, but his leadership of the Blue Dog dark-money infrastructure suggests the talk may be cheaper than the price of admission to WelcomeFest. Voters who care about the 80% Bill's agenda should watch what he builds, not just what he says.

Adelita S. Grijalva is the representative for Arizona's 7th Congressional District, the first Latina to represent Arizona in Congress, and the daughter of the late Rep. Raúl Grijalva, who held the seat for over two decades as a progressive lion [1]. Before arriving in Washington, she served eighteen years on the Tucson Unified School District Governing Board—the youngest woman ever elected to it—and later won a seat on the Pima County Board of Supervisors [2]. She won a September 2025 special election to succeed her father, then waited seven weeks while Speaker Mike Johnson delayed her swearing-in, a stall widely seen as an effort to block a vote on releasing the Epstein files [3]. On the day she was finally sworn in, she signed the discharge petition as the decisive 218th signature, forcing that very vote [4].

Grijalva is one of the more naturally aligned members of Congress with the 80% Bill's agenda. She campaigned on Medicare for All, joined the Expand Social Security Caucus, and cosponsored legislation to eliminate the Social Security payroll tax cap—directly touching Bills 7, 11, and 4 [5]. She supports banning Wall Street from buying up neighborhoods, echoing Bill 10, and has championed paid family leave and affordable childcare, mirroring Bill 14 [6]. As a Congressional Progressive Caucus member endorsed by Bernie Sanders, she sits comfortably in the camp that has long called for overturning Citizens United (Bill 13), passing the DISCLOSE Act (Bill 16), and ending dark money in politics (Bill 5) [7]. Her father was famous for pushing a revolving-door lobbying ban on executive branch nominees, a torch she appears ready to carry [8].

In her first week in office, Grijalva cosponsored 56 bills—a record pace that included the Protecting Healthcare and Lowering Cost Act, the Women's Health Protection Act, and the Child Care for Working Families Act [9]. She introduced the No Delay in Representation Act, born from her own experience being frozen out by Johnson's parliamentary gamesmanship [10]. On the 80% Bill scorecard, her positions align with a clear majority of the twenty items: stock trading bans, Pentagon audits, term limits, and anti-gerrymandering measures all fit neatly within the progressive caucus platform she has embraced.

Follow the money, and you find relatively little to follow. Grijalva raised roughly $835,000 for her special election, with a late $120,000 surge that erased a fundraising gap, and she does not appear on OpenSecrets with a member ID—a reflection of her newcomer status rather than any opacity [11]. Her endorsements came from the Congressional Progressive Caucus, the League of Conservation Voters, Our Revolution, and a constellation of labor and environmental groups rather than corporate PACs [7][12]. In a Congress awash in dark money, that is either admirable restraint or the luxury of representing a safely blue district where grassroots cash suffices.

The bottom line on Grijalva is that she is about as close to an 80% Bill co-sponsor-in-waiting as the House produces. The contradictions here are not between her positions and popular will, but between her ambitions and the arithmetic of a Republican-controlled chamber. She has the right votes on paper; whether she can turn any of them into law from the minority is the question that separates legacy from legislation.

John Hickenlooper is Colorado's senior senator, a Democrat who arrived in Washington in 2021 after two terms as governor and a brief, forgettable presidential campaign. A geologist-turned-brewpub-owner-turned-politician, Hickenlooper has built his brand on genial centrism and a talent for making corporate-friendly governance sound like folksy common sense. In the Senate, he sits on the Commerce, Energy, and Health committees, and has emerged as one of the chamber's most prolific bipartisan dealmakers -- or, depending on your perspective, one of its most reliable Democratic votes for the Trump administration's cabinet picks [1][2].

On paper, Hickenlooper checks a surprising number of boxes on the 80% Bill. He co-sponsored the For the People Act, which would overhaul campaign finance, crack down on dark money, slow the lobbying revolving door, and end gerrymandering [3]. He backed the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a provision he publicly championed, hosting town halls with AARP to celebrate lower prescription costs [4]. He introduced the Keep Billionaires Out of Social Security Act [5] and voted against Republican budget plans that would give tax breaks to the ultra-wealthy [6]. He supported the Epstein Files Transparency Act, co-authoring the Senate Democrats' demand for full public release [7]. On the surface, this is a senator who should be a natural champion for the populist, anti-corruption reforms that 80% of Americans want.

But the contradictions pile up fast. When the Inflation Reduction Act was being negotiated, the carried interest loophole -- a tax dodge beloved by hedge fund managers -- was on the chopping block. Senator Sinema killed it, and Hickenlooper's response was not to fight for the closure but to propose an alternative revenue source, letting Wall Street keep its favorite loophole intact [8]. He co-sponsored a stock trading ban for Congress in the 119th session, yet in his first year as senator he was an active stock trader himself, filing seven periodic transaction reports and unloading over $4 million in tech stocks including Amazon, Apple, and Alphabet [9]. In September 2025, while serving on the Commerce Committee, he invested up to $250,000 in cybersecurity firm Palo Alto Networks [9]. And as governor, he was fined $2,750 by Colorado's Independent Ethics Commission for accepting gifts including a private jet ride from homebuilder Larry Mizel and luxury perks at the Bilderberg Meetings in Italy [10].

Follow the money and the picture sharpens. Hickenlooper rejected corporate PAC money during his 2020 Senate campaign, earning endorsements from End Citizens United [11]. But his top industry donors have included securities and investment firms, real estate interests, and lawyers and lobbyists -- the very sectors that benefit most from the carried interest loophole he declined to fight for and the revolving door he claims to oppose [12]. His voting record in 2025 tells its own story: he confirmed 10 of Trump's cabinet nominees, more than any Democrat except John Fetterman, voting with the GOP roughly a quarter of the time while representing a state that went for Kamala Harris by 11 points [1][2]. His explanation -- that confirming Trump's picks "reduced the damage" by building relationships -- is the kind of Washington logic that makes voters reach for the cynicism [2].

The bottom line on Hickenlooper is a familiar one in American politics: a senator who talks the populist talk on drug prices and dark money while quietly accommodating the financial class on taxes and stock trading, and cozying up to whichever administration holds power. He is not the worst offender in the Senate by a long stretch. But for a Democrat representing a blue state, the gap between his rhetoric on corruption and his personal financial maneuvering is precisely the kind of thing the 80% Bill was designed to address.

Bill Hagerty is a Republican senator from Tennessee who took office in 2021 after serving as Donald Trump's Ambassador to Japan. Before entering politics, Hagerty co-founded Hagerty Peterson & Company, a private equity firm, amassing an estimated net worth of around $55 million — making him one of the wealthiest members of Congress [1]. He sits on the Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee, the Foreign Relations Committee, and the Appropriations Committee [2]. It's a tidy arrangement for a man whose fortune was built on the very financial markets his committee oversees.

On the subject of stock trading, Hagerty's record speaks volumes. In 2022, he was found to have violated the STOCK Act by failing to disclose on time the sale of Signature Bank stock from three of his dependent children's trusts, transactions worth between $45,000 and $150,000 [3]. The trades were made in December 2021 but not reported until July 2022 — roughly six months past the 45-day deadline. Despite this personal brush with the transparency law, Hagerty has not cosponsored any of the bipartisan bills to ban congressional stock trading outright. He voted against the DISCLOSE Act in September 2022, which would have required disclosure of dark money donors, calling it a threat to "free speech" [4]. The Pentagon, meanwhile, has failed seven consecutive audits [5], but Hagerty is notably absent from the bipartisan Audit the Pentagon Act led by Senators Sanders and Grassley. He has not signed on to support term limits legislation either.

Where Hagerty has been enthusiastic is cryptocurrency. From his perch on the Banking Committee, he introduced the GENIUS Act to create a regulatory framework for stablecoins [6] and has championed pro-Bitcoin legislation, declaring that voters should elect candidates who support digital assets [7]. This is a curious priority for a senator whose own financial disclosures show up to $53.8 million in stock trades parsed from STOCK Act filings [8]. One might wonder whether the senator's passion for deregulating digital finance has anything to do with his own extensive investment portfolio.

Hagerty's campaign coffers reflect his financial-sector roots. His 2020 Senate run drew at least $28,000 from members of a White House economic advisory panel he served on, plus $17,000 from corporate PACs including Bank of America [9]. His fundraisers featured Donald Trump Jr. and Nikki Haley [10]. He has not supported banning corporate PACs, lobbying reform, Medicare drug price negotiation, paid family leave, or any of the other broadly popular policies in the 80% Bill. For a man who built his career in private equity and now regulates the financial industry from a Senate committee, the alignment between his donors, his investments, and his legislative priorities is, at the very least, remarkably consistent.
